question: If vector a cross vector b = vector c and vector b cross vector c = vector a, then
question_latex: If $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{c}$ and $\vec{b} \times \vec{c} = \vec{a}$, then
solution:
$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{c}$ implies $\vec{c}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{a}$ and $\vec{b}$, and $\vec{b} \times \vec{c} = \vec{a}$ implies $\vec{a}$ is perpendicular to $\vec{b}$ and $\vec{c}$. So $\vec{a}, \vec{b}, \vec{c}$ are mutually perpendicular.
From $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = \vec{c}$: $|\vec{a}||\vec{b}|\sin 90° = |\vec{c}|$ ... (i)
From $\vec{b} \times \vec{c} = \vec{a}$: $|\vec{b}||\vec{c}|\sin 90° = |\vec{a}|$ ... (ii)
From (i) and (ii): $|\vec{b}|^2|\vec{c}| = |\vec{c}|$, so $|\vec{b}|^2 = 1$ ($\because |\vec{c}| \neq 0$)
$\Rightarrow |\vec{b}| = 1$ and $|\vec{a}| = |\vec{c}|$

question: If vector a = (1, p, 1), vector b = (q, 2, 2), vector a dot vector b = r and vector a cross vector b = (0, -3, 3), then p, q, r are in that order
question_latex: If $\vec{a} = (1, p, 1)$, $\vec{b} = (q, 2, 2)$, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = r$ and $\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (0, -3, 3)$, then $p, q, r$ are in that order
solution:
$\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = q + 2p + 2 = r$ ... (i)
$\vec{a} \times \vec{b} = (2p-2)\hat{i} + (q-2)\hat{j} + (2-pq)\hat{k} = (0\hat{i} + (-3)\hat{j} + (3)\hat{k})$
$\Rightarrow 2p-2=0; q-2=-3; 2-pq=3$
$\Rightarrow p=1, q=-1$
From (i): $-1+2+2 = r = 3$
So $p=1, q=-1, r=3$, which is none of the given options.
